**#4226** 10:09 PM 🔵 **Dependency vulnerability audit completed with moderate-severity findings only**

The Kody Watch agent executed pnpm audit to scan the repository's 28 dependencies for known security vulnerabilities. The audit identified 8 moderate-severity advisories affecting 5 packages: esbuild (CORS bypass in dev server), ajv (ReDoS with $data option), file-type (ASF parser infinite loop), dompurify (multiple XSS variants including mutation-XSS and prototype pollution), and vite (WebSocket fetchModule file exposure). All identified vulnerabilities have available patches: esbuild ≥0.25.0, ajv ≥8.18.0, file-type ≥21.3.1, dompurify ≥3.3.2, and vite ≥7.3.2. However, since the watch instructions specify creating GitHub issues only for high and critical severity vulnerabilities, and this audit found none at those levels, no issues will be created. The moderate vulnerabilities represent development-time risks rather than production threats, as they primarily affect the dev server (esbuild, vite) or require specific usage patterns (ajv with $data, dompurify with specific configurations).

Read: ~437, Work: 🔍 13,591

**General**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4228** " ⚖️ **Memory Benchmark Protocol Configured for Kody Engine Testing**

A comprehensive memory benchmark protocol was configured as a weekly watch agent for the Kody Engine testing repository (aharonyaircohen/Kody-Engine-Tester). The benchmark measures how effectively the Kody memory system handles accumulated context across multiple issues. It creates 20 test issues spanning typical development tasks: utility functions (clamp, slugify, retry, deep merge, debounce), authentication helpers (token expiry, password validation, session cleanup, rate limiting, role hierarchy), service components (pagination, search filters, notifications, caching, audit logging), and mixed components (breadcrumbs, middleware, API endpoints, CSV export, input sanitization). The protocol tracks six key metrics: M1 measures token compression by comparing promptTokens across pipeline stages as memory accumulates; M2 tracks diary growth by counting entries after each batch; M3 validates room scoping by counting room-specific convention files created; M4 tests contradiction detection by checking for repeat failure warnings; M5 measures deduplication effectiveness via conventions.md line count growth rate; M6 tracks retry reduction to verify later runs benefit from accumulated diary context. Intentional failure scenarios test the system's ability to detect contradictions when broken code is rerun without fixes. The structured execution ensures consistent measurement: each batch records baseline metrics, creates all issues in parallel, triggers them simultaneously, polls for completion with timeouts, captures post-batch state, cleans up temporary artifacts, then proceeds to the next batch. Results compile into a structured markdown report showing per-batch token usage, diary growth patterns, room file creation, contradiction detection effectiveness, conventions file growth, and retry count trends.

Read: ~734, Work: ⚖️ 6,166

**package.json**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4227** " 🔵 **Two high-severity vulnerabilities identified in vite package**

The agent refined the vulnerability analysis by running a Python script to parse the pnpm audit JSON output and filter specifically for high and critical severity vulnerabilities. This filtered analysis revealed two high-severity security issues in vite 7.3.1, both related to file access controls in the development server. The first vulnerability (advisory 1116100, GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583) allows bypassing server.fs checks through the WebSocket-exposed fetchModule method, while the second (advisory 1116126, GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r) enables bypassing server.fs.deny restrictions to return file contents to browsers. Both vulnerabilities only affect applications that explicitly expose the Vite dev server to the network and have WebSocket enabled. The agent will now check for existing GitHub issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label before creating new issue reports for these findings.

Read: ~386, Work: 🔍 1,958

**General**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4229** 10:10 PM 🔵 **Existing vulnerability issues prevent duplicate creation**

The agent queried the repository for existing open issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label to prevent duplicate issue creation. The search revealed two existing vulnerability issues: #493 for vite (HIGH severity) and #353 for ajv (HIGH severity). Since the current audit identified high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1, but an open vite vulnerability issue already exists (#493), the agent will skip creating new issues per the Kody Watch guidelines that state "if there is already an open issue with label kody:watch:vulnerability mentioning the package name, skip it." This deduplication mechanism prevents cluttering the issue tracker with redundant vulnerability reports while the existing issues remain open and unresolved.

Read: ~337, Work: 🔍 583

**General**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4230** " 🔵 **Existing issue 493 already documents both current vite vulnerabilities**

The agent inspected the contents of existing issue #493 to verify whether it covers the vulnerabilities discovered in the current audit. The issue body documents both high-severity vite vulnerabilities found in today's scan: GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r (server.fs.deny bypass with queries) and GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583 (arbitrary file read via WebSocket). Both entries specify vite 7.3.1 as the current vulnerable version, identify >=7.3.2 as the patched version, and provide the upgrade command. Since the existing issue perfectly matches the current audit findings, creating new issues would produce exact duplicates. The Kody Watch vulnerability monitoring workflow successfully detected this overlap and avoided cluttering the issue tracker, allowing maintainers to focus on the single existing issue for remediation.

Read: ~356, Work: 🔍 1,306

**#S2285** Automated vulnerability audit of Kody-Engine-Tester repository dependencies (Apr 7 at 10:10 PM)

**Investigated**: The Kody Watch agent audited the repository's dependency stack by reading package.json (28 dependencies across Next.js/PayloadCMS stack), confirming pnpm package manager usage via pnpm-lock.yaml, and running pnpm audit to scan for known security vulnerabilities. The audit identified 8 moderate-severity vulnerabilities across esbuild, ajv, file-type, dompurify, and vite, plus 2 high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1 (GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583 for WebSocket arbitrary file read and GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r for server.fs.deny bypass). The agent then queried existing GitHub issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label and inspected issue #493 to verify coverage.

**Learned**: The vulnerability audit workflow uses pnpm audit with JSON output filtering to isolate high and critical severity findings. Vite 7.3.1 contains two high-severity file exposure vulnerabilities affecting development servers exposed to networks, both patched in version 7.3.2. The deduplication mechanism checks existing open issues by package name before creating new vulnerability reports. Issue #493 already documents both current vite vulnerabilities with identical advisory URLs, affected versions, and upgrade guidance (pnpm update vite@>=7.3.2), making new issue creation unnecessary.

**Completed**: Dependency vulnerability audit completed for watch cycle #1. Scanned 28 dependencies, identified 2 high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1 (both file access control bypasses), verified existing issue #493 covers both findings, and determined no new GitHub issues required per Kody Watch deduplication rules.

**Next Steps**: Audit cycle complete. No new issues to create since existing issue #493 covers all high/critical vulnerabilities found. The agent will await the next scheduled watch cycle.

**#4226** 10:09 PM 🔵 **Dependency vulnerability audit completed with moderate-severity findings only**

The Kody Watch agent executed pnpm audit to scan the repository's 28 dependencies for known security vulnerabilities. The audit identified 8 moderate-severity advisories affecting 5 packages: esbuild (CORS bypass in dev server), ajv (ReDoS with $data option), file-type (ASF parser infinite loop), dompurify (multiple XSS variants including mutation-XSS and prototype pollution), and vite (WebSocket fetchModule file exposure). All identified vulnerabilities have available patches: esbuild ≥0.25.0, ajv ≥8.18.0, file-type ≥21.3.1, dompurify ≥3.3.2, and vite ≥7.3.2. However, since the watch instructions specify creating GitHub issues only for high and critical severity vulnerabilities, and this audit found none at those levels, no issues will be created. The moderate vulnerabilities represent development-time risks rather than production threats, as they primarily affect the dev server (esbuild, vite) or require specific usage patterns (ajv with $data, dompurify with specific configurations).

Read: ~437, Work: 🔍 13,591

**General**
| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |
|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4228** " ⚖️ **Memory Benchmark Protocol Configured for Kody Engine Testing**

A comprehensive memory benchmark protocol was configured as a weekly watch agent for the Kody Engine testing repository (aharonyaircohen/Kody-Engine-Tester). The benchmark measures how effectively the Kody memory system handles accumulated context across multiple issues. It creates 20 test issues spanning typical development tasks: utility functions (clamp, slugify, retry, deep merge, debounce), authentication helpers (token expiry, password validation, session cleanup, rate limiting, role hierarchy), service components (pagination, search filters, notifications, caching, audit logging), and mixed components (breadcrumbs, middleware, API endpoints, CSV export, input sanitization). The protocol tracks six key metrics: M1 measures token compression by comparing promptTokens across pipeline stages as memory accumulates; M2 tracks diary growth by counting entries after each batch; M3 validates room scoping by counting room-specific convention files created; M4 tests contradiction detection by checking for repeat failure warnings; M5 measures deduplication effectiveness via conventions.md line count growth rate; M6 tracks retry reduction to verify later runs benefit from accumulated diary context. Intentional failure scenarios test the system's ability to detect contradictions when broken code is rerun without fixes. The structured execution ensures consistent measurement: each batch records baseline metrics, creates all issues in parallel, triggers them simultaneously, polls for completion with timeouts, captures post-batch state, cleans up temporary artifacts, then proceeds to the next batch. Results compile into a structured markdown report showing per-batch token usage, diary growth patterns, room file creation, contradiction detection effectiveness, conventions file growth, and retry count trends.

Read: ~734, Work: ⚖️ 6,166

**package.json**

|----|------|---|-------|------|------|

**#4227** " 🔵 **Two high-severity vulnerabilities identified in vite package**

The agent refined the vulnerability analysis by running a Python script to parse the pnpm audit JSON output and filter specifically for high and critical severity vulnerabilities. This filtered analysis revealed two high-severity security issues in vite 7.3.1, both related to file access controls in the development server. The first vulnerability (advisory 1116100, GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583) allows bypassing server.fs checks through the WebSocket-exposed fetchModule method, while the second (advisory 1116126, GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r) enables bypassing server.fs.deny restrictions to return file contents to browsers. Both vulnerabilities only affect applications that explicitly expose the Vite dev server to the network and have WebSocket enabled. The agent will now check for existing GitHub issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label before creating new issue reports for these findings.

Read: ~386, Work: 🔍 1,958

**#4229** 10:10 PM 🔵 **Existing vulnerability issues prevent duplicate creation**

The agent queried the repository for existing open issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label to prevent duplicate issue creation. The search revealed two existing vulnerability issues: #493 for vite (HIGH severity) and #353 for ajv (HIGH severity). Since the current audit identified high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1, but an open vite vulnerability issue already exists (#493), the agent will skip creating new issues per the Kody Watch guidelines that state "if there is already an open issue with label kody:watch:vulnerability mentioning the package name, skip it." This deduplication mechanism prevents cluttering the issue tracker with redundant vulnerability reports while the existing issues remain open and unresolved.

Read: ~337, Work: 🔍 583

| ID | Time | T | Title | Read | Work |

**#4230** " 🔵 **Existing issue 493 already documents both current vite vulnerabilities**

The agent inspected the contents of existing issue #493 to verify whether it covers the vulnerabilities discovered in the current audit. The issue body documents both high-severity vite vulnerabilities found in today's scan: GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r (server.fs.deny bypass with queries) and GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583 (arbitrary file read via WebSocket). Both entries specify vite 7.3.1 as the current vulnerable version, identify >=7.3.2 as the patched version, and provide the upgrade command. Since the existing issue perfectly matches the current audit findings, creating new issues would produce exact duplicates. The Kody Watch vulnerability monitoring workflow successfully detected this overlap and avoided cluttering the issue tracker, allowing maintainers to focus on the single existing issue for remediation.

Read: ~356, Work: 🔍 1,306

**#S2285** Automated vulnerability audit of Kody-Engine-Tester repository dependencies (Apr 7 at 10:10 PM)

**Investigated**: The Kody Watch agent audited the repository's dependency stack by reading package.json (28 dependencies across Next.js/PayloadCMS stack), confirming pnpm package manager usage via pnpm-lock.yaml, and running pnpm audit to scan for known security vulnerabilities. The audit identified 8 moderate-severity vulnerabilities across esbuild, ajv, file-type, dompurify, and vite, plus 2 high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1 (GHSA-p9ff-h696-f583 for WebSocket arbitrary file read and GHSA-v2wj-q39q-566r for server.fs.deny bypass). The agent then queried existing GitHub issues with the kody:watch:vulnerability label and inspected issue #493 to verify coverage.

**Learned**: The vulnerability audit workflow uses pnpm audit with JSON output filtering to isolate high and critical severity findings. Vite 7.3.1 contains two high-severity file exposure vulnerabilities affecting development servers exposed to networks, both patched in version 7.3.2. The deduplication mechanism checks existing open issues by package name before creating new vulnerability reports. Issue #493 already documents both current vite vulnerabilities with identical advisory URLs, affected versions, and upgrade guidance (pnpm update vite@>=7.3.2), making new issue creation unnecessary.

**Completed**: Dependency vulnerability audit completed for watch cycle #1. Scanned 28 dependencies, identified 2 high-severity vulnerabilities in vite 7.3.1 (both file access control bypasses), verified existing issue #493 covers both findings, and determined no new GitHub issues required per Kody Watch deduplication rules.

**Next Steps**: Audit cycle complete. No new issues to create since existing issue #493 covers all high/critical vulnerabilities found. The agent will await the next scheduled watch cycle.
